title: Normalize & Deduplicate Usernames
description: Given list of usernames, normalize by: lowercase, remove dots ., ignore everything after + (like email rules), then count unique. Example: ["a.b+c", "ab", "A.B"] -> unique normalized = 1.

examples.explanation:
Step 1: Process "a.b+c" → lowercase: "a.b+c" → remove dots: "ab+c" → truncate after '+': "ab" → normalized = "ab" → seen_normalized = [] (empty) → no matches → unique_count = 1, seen_normalized = ["ab"]
Step 2: Process "ab" → lowercase: "ab" → remove dots: "ab" → truncate after '+': "ab" (no '+') → normalized = "ab" → compare with seen_normalized ["ab"] → match found → skip
Step 3: Process "A.B" → lowercase: "a.b" → remove dots: "ab" → truncate after '+': "ab" (no '+') → normalized = "ab" → compare with seen_normalized ["ab"] → match found → skip
Final: unique_count = 1

Step 1: Process "test.email+alex@leetcode.com" → lowercase: "test.email+alex@leetcode.com" → remove dots: "testemail+alex@leetcode.com" → truncate after '+': "testemail" → normalized = "testemail" → unique_count = 1, seen = ["testemail"]
Step 2: Process "test.e.mail+bob.cathy@leetcode.com" → lowercase: same → remove dots: "testemail+bobcathy@leetcode.com" → truncate after '+': "testemail" → match found → skip
Step 3: Process "testemail+david@lee.tcode.com" → lowercase: same → no dots to remove → truncate after '+': "testemail" → match found → skip
Final: unique_count = 1

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n^2 * m)
approaches.summary: Iterate through each username, manually apply normalization rules (lowercase, remove dots, truncate after +), and check for duplicates by comparing against all previously processed usernames. Use nested loops to compare each normalized username with others to count unique ones.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) if not storing normalized usernames, or O(n * m) if storing them

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Normalization and Deduplication
ladders.desc: Complete solution using nested loops to normalize usernames and count unique ones.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the brute force approach. The intuition is straightforward: process each username individually, apply all normalization rules, then compare it against every other normalized username to check for duplicates. We'll use two nested loops - the outer loop picks each username to process, and the inner loop compares it against all previously processed usernames. For each username, we first normalize it by: 1) converting to lowercase, 2) removing all '.' characters, and 3) truncating everything after the first '+' character (including the '+'). Then we check if this normalized version already exists among previously processed usernames. If not, we increment our unique count and add it to our list of seen normalized usernames. The time complexity is O(n² * m) where n is the number of usernames and m is the average username length, because for each of n usernames, we compare against up to n other usernames, and each comparison takes O(m) time. Space complexity is O(n * m) if we store all normalized usernames, or O(1) if we only store a count (but then we can't properly compare). This connects to L1 by using the normalization function as a key subroutine. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle the '+' truncation correctly (should remove everything after '+', not just the '+'), not considering that usernames might be identical after normalization even if different before, and inefficient comparison logic that doesn't properly track which usernames have already been processed. Real code pattern: Initialize unique_count = 0 and seen_normalized = empty list. For each username in input list: normalized = normalize(username). For each seen_norm in seen_normalized: if normalized == seen_norm: skip to next username. If no match found: increment unique_count and append normalized to seen_normalized. Return unique_count.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Process "a.b+c" → lowercase: "a.b+c" → remove dots: "ab+c" → truncate after '+': "ab" → normalized = "ab" → seen_normalized = [] (empty) → no matches → unique_count = 1, seen_normalized = ["ab"]
Step 2: Process "ab" → lowercase: "ab" → remove dots: "ab" → truncate after '+': "ab" (no '+') → normalized = "ab" → compare with seen_normalized ["ab"] → match found → skip
Step 3: Process "A.B" → lowercase: "a.b" → remove dots: "ab" → truncate after '+': "ab" (no '+') → normalized = "ab" → compare with seen_normalized ["ab"] → match found → skip
Final: unique_count = 1

Step 1: Process "test.email+alex@leetcode.com" → lowercase: "test.email+alex@leetcode.com" → remove dots: "testemail+alex@leetcode.com" → truncate after '+': "testemail" → normalized = "testemail" → seen_normalized = [] → unique_count = 1, seen_normalized = ["testemail"]
Step 2: Process "test.e.mail+bob.cathy@leetcode.com" → lowercase: same → remove dots: "testemail+bobcathy@leetcode.com" → truncate after '+': "testemail" → normalized = "testemail" → compare with ["testemail"] → match found → skip
Step 3: Process "testemail+david@lee.tcode.com" → lowercase: same → no dots to remove → truncate after '+': "testemail" → normalized = "testemail" → compare with ["testemail"] → match found → skip
Final: unique_count = 1

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Normalization Function
ladders.desc: Function to apply all normalization rules to a single username.
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function that transforms a raw username into its normalized form. The intuition is that we need to consistently apply three rules to any username string: 1) Convert all characters to lowercase to eliminate case differences, 2) Remove all '.' characters since they're ignored in the normalization, and 3) Truncate the string at the first '+' character (removing the '+' and everything after it). These rules mimic email address normalization for the local part. The algorithm works sequentially: first, we convert the entire string to lowercase using a built-in function or by iterating through characters. Then, we iterate through the lowercase string and build a new string, skipping all '.' characters. Finally, we scan this dot-removed string until we find a '+' character; if found, we only keep the prefix before it. If no '+' is found, we keep the entire dot-removed string. Time complexity is O(m) where m is the length of the username, since we make at most two passes through the string (one for lowercase conversion and one for processing dots and plus). Space complexity is O(m) for the new normalized string. This connects to L0 as the core transformation used in the main algorithm. Common mistakes include: not handling the '+' truncation correctly (should remove everything after the first '+', not just remove '+' characters), forgetting that the truncation should happen after dot removal (order matters), and inefficient string concatenation. Real code pattern: Define function normalize(username): result = empty string. Convert username to lowercase (or process char by char). For each char in lowercase_username: if char == '.': continue (skip). Else if char == '+': break (stop processing). Else: append char to result. Return result.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Input = "a.b+c"
Step 2: Convert to lowercase: "a.b+c" (already lowercase)
Step 3: Process characters:
- 'a': not '.' or '+' → append → result = "a"
- '.': is '.' → skip
- 'b': not '.' or '+' → append → result = "ab"
- '+': is '+' → break processing
Step 4: Return "ab"

Step 1: Input = "A.B"
Step 2: Convert to lowercase: "a.b"
Step 3: Process characters:
- 'a': not '.' or '+' → append → result = "a"
- '.': is '.' → skip
- 'b': not '.' or '+' → append → result = "ab"
Step 4: No '+' found, so return "ab"

ladders.title: Core Logic — Duplicate Detection with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Using nested loops to compare each normalized username against others.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique of the brute force approach: detecting duplicates by comparing each element against all others. The intuition is simple but powerful - if we want to know if an item is unique, we can check if it appears anywhere else in the collection. We maintain a list of normalized usernames that we've already identified as unique. For each new normalized username, we compare it against every username in this 'seen' list. If we find a match, it's a duplicate; if we check all and find no match, it's unique and we add it to the list. The algorithm uses two nested loops: the outer loop iterates through each input username (after normalization), and the inner loop compares it against all previously seen unique normalized usernames. We need to be careful about the order - we process usernames sequentially, and only compare against those we've already processed and deemed unique. Time complexity is O(n²) for the comparisons (where n is the number of usernames), plus O(n * m) for the normalizations. Space complexity is O(n * m) for storing the seen normalized usernames. This connects to L0 as the main duplicate detection mechanism, and to L1 by using the normalized versions for comparison. Common mistakes include: comparing against all usernames including future ones (which causes redundant comparisons), not resetting the duplicate flag correctly for each new username, and inefficient comparison order. Real code pattern: seen = empty list. For each normalized_username in normalized_list: is_duplicate = False. For each seen_username in seen: if normalized_username == seen_username: is_duplicate = True; break. If not is_duplicate: append normalized_username to seen. Finally, length of seen is the unique count.

examples.trace:
Assume these are already normalized usernames.
Step 1: Process "ab" → seen = [] → no comparisons → is_duplicate = False → append "ab" to seen → seen = ["ab"]
Step 2: Process "ab" → seen = ["ab"] → compare with "ab" → match found → is_duplicate = True → break → don't append
Step 3: Process "cd" → seen = ["ab"] → compare with "ab" → no match → is_duplicate = False → append "cd" to seen → seen = ["ab", "cd"]
Final: unique count = length of seen = 2

Step 1: Process "a" → seen = [] → no match → append → seen = ["a"]
Step 2: Process "b" → compare with "a" → no match → append → seen = ["a", "b"]
Step 3: Process "c" → compare with "a" (no), "b" (no) → append → seen = ["a", "b", "c"]
Step 4: Process "a" → compare with "a" → match → duplicate → don't append
Step 5: Process "b" → compare with "a" (no), "b" (match) → duplicate → don't append
Final: unique count = 3

ladders.title: Building Block — String Processing with Character Rules
ladders.desc: Processing a string while applying conditional rules to each character.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves iterating through a string and building a new string based on rules applied to each character. The intuition is that we need to transform an input string into an output string by examining each character and deciding whether to include it, modify it, or stop processing. This is a fundamental pattern in string manipulation problems. The algorithm typically involves: initializing an empty result string, iterating through each character of the input string, applying conditional logic to each character, and appending to the result string based on the rules. For our normalization problem, the rules are: include lowercase letters, skip '.' characters, and stop processing when encountering a '+' character. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the string length, since we examine each character once. Space complexity is O(n) for the new string. This connects to L1 as the implementation technique for the normalization function, and to L4 which handles individual character operations. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in loop indices, incorrect order of condition checks (e.g., checking for '+' after appending), and inefficient string concatenation in languages where strings are immutable. Real code pattern: result = "". For i from 0 to length(input_string)-1: char = input_string[i]. If char == '.': continue. Else if char == '+': break. Else: result = result + char (or use StringBuilder). Return result.

examples.trace:
Input: "a.b"
Initialize result = ""
Index 0: char = 'a' → not '.' or '+' → append → result = "a"
Index 1: char = '.' → is '.' → skip (continue)
Index 2: char = 'b' → not '.' or '+' → append → result = "ab"
End of string → return "ab"

Input: "test+more"
Initialize result = ""
Index 0: char = 't' → append → result = "t"
Index 1: char = 'e' → append → result = "te"
Index 2: char = 's' → append → result = "tes"
Index 3: char = 't' → append → result = "test"
Index 4: char = '+' → is '+' → break loop
Return "test"

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Classification and Transformation
ladders.desc: Identifying character types and performing basic transformations.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves examining individual characters and determining their properties (like whether they're a letter, digit, punctuation, or specific characters like '.' or '+'), and performing simple transformations like converting to lowercase. The intuition is that before we can apply rules to characters, we need to recognize what type of character we're dealing with. For our problem, we need to: 1) Identify if a character is a '.' (period) to skip it, 2) Identify if a character is a '+' (plus) to stop processing, and 3) Convert uppercase letters to lowercase for inclusion in the result. The algorithm for a single character is straightforward: check its value against specific characters or ranges. For lowercase conversion, we can use built-in functions or ASCII arithmetic: if char is between 'A' and 'Z', add 32 to convert to lowercase. Time complexity is O(1) per character. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as the atomic operation performed on each character during string processing. Common mistakes include: forgetting that character comparisons are case-sensitive ('.' is different from maybe other representations), incorrect ASCII arithmetic for non-alphabetic characters, and not handling edge cases like empty strings or special characters. Real code pattern: For a given character c: if c == '.': return SKIP. else if c == '+': return STOP. else: if c is uppercase letter: return lowercase version of c. else: return c.

examples.trace:
Input character: 'A'
Step 1: Check if it's '.' → no
Step 2: Check if it's '+' → no
Step 3: Check if it's uppercase letter → yes ('A' is between 'A' and 'Z')
Step 4: Convert to lowercase: 'A' has ASCII 65, lowercase 'a' has ASCII 97, difference is 32 → 65 + 32 = 97 → character 'a'
Output: 'a'

Input character: '.'
Step 1: Check if it's '.' → yes
Output: SKIP (special signal to skip this character)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Iterating Through a List
ladders.desc: Basic loop to process each element in a collection.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: how to iterate through a list or array to access each element one by one. The intuition is that to process multiple items, we need a way to systematically visit each item in order. This is typically done with a loop construct that has a counter (index) that goes from the first position to the last. The algorithm is: initialize an index variable to 0 (for zero-based indexing), while the index is less than the length of the list, access the element at that index, perform some operation on it, then increment the index. Alternatively, many languages provide for-each loops that handle the indexing automatically. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of elements, since we visit each once. Space complexity is O(1) for the loop counter. This connects to L2 and L0 as the fundamental way to access each username for processing. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors (going one index too far or not far enough), modifying the list while iterating (which can cause unexpected behavior), and confusing the element value with the index. Real code pattern: For i from 0 to length(list)-1: element = list[i]; process(element). Or: For each element in list: process(element).

examples.trace:
List has 3 elements.
Method 1: Using index loop:
Initialize i = 0
i=0: element = list[0] = "apple" → process "apple"
i=1: element = list[1] = "banana" → process "banana"
i=2: element = list[2] = "cherry" → process "cherry"
i=3: i is not < 3, so loop ends.
Method 2: Using for-each:
Take "apple" → process "apple"
Take "banana" → process "banana"
Take "cherry" → process "cherry"
Done.

List has 3 numbers.
Using index loop with i from 0 to 2:
i=0: list[0] = 10
i=1: list[1] = 20
i=2: list[2] = 30
Loop ends.

approaches.name: Hash Set O(n * m)
approaches.summary: Normalize each username by applying the rules (lowercase, remove dots, ignore after +) and store the results in a hash set to automatically deduplicate. The size of the set gives the count of unique normalized usernames.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Unique Normalized Usernames
ladders.desc: Given a list of usernames, normalize each by applying email-like rules and count unique results.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires processing a list of usernames to count how many are unique after normalization. The normalization rules mimic email address processing: convert to lowercase, remove all dot (.) characters, and ignore everything after a plus (+) sign (including the plus). The intuition is that these transformations reveal the 'true' identity behind various formatting variations. The algorithm processes each username through a normalization function, then uses a hash set to automatically deduplicate the results. The size of the set gives the count of unique normalized usernames. Time complexity is O(n * m) where n is number of usernames and m is average length, as we process each character. Space complexity is O(n * m) to store normalized strings in the set. This connects to L1 by using the normalization function as a key subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle empty strings, not removing dots before processing plus signs, or incorrectly handling multiple plus signs. Real code pattern: def normalize(username): username = username.lower(); username = username.replace('.', ''); if '+' in username: username = username[:username.index('+')]; return username. Then: set = {}; for u in usernames: set.add(normalize(u)); return len(set).

examples.trace: Step 1: Process "a.b+c" -> lowercase: "a.b+c" -> remove dots: "ab+c" -> ignore after +: "ab" -> normalized: "ab". Step 2: Process "ab" -> lowercase: "ab" -> no dots to remove -> no + to ignore -> normalized: "ab". Step 3: Process "A.B" -> lowercase: "a.b" -> remove dots: "ab" -> no + to ignore -> normalized: "ab". Step 4: Add to set: {"ab"}. Step 5: Count unique: 1.

Step 1: Process "test.email+alex@leetcode.com" -> lowercase: "test.email+alex@leetcode.com" -> remove dots: "testemail+alex@leetcode.com" -> ignore after +: "testemail" -> normalized: "testemail". Step 2: Process "test.e.mail+bob.cathy@leetcode.com" -> lowercase: same -> remove dots: "testemail+bobcathy@leetcode.com" -> ignore after +: "testemail" -> normalized: "testemail" (already in set). Step 3: Process "testemail+david@lee.tcode.com" -> lowercase: same -> no dots to remove -> ignore after +: "testemail" -> normalized: "testemail" (already in set). Set: {"testemail"}. Count: 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Normalize a Single Username
ladders.desc: Implement a function that normalizes one username according to the given rules.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the full problem: transforming a raw username into its normalized form. The intuition is that we need to systematically apply three string transformations in sequence: first convert to lowercase to eliminate case differences, then remove all dot characters to consolidate separated parts, and finally truncate at the first plus sign (if any) to ignore suffixes. This order matters because dots might appear after the plus sign, but since we ignore everything after plus, we should remove dots before handling plus to ensure all dots in the relevant part are removed. The algorithm: 1. Convert the string to lowercase using a built-in function or manual iteration. 2. Remove all occurrences of the '.' character by building a new string without dots. 3. Find the index of the first '+' character; if found, truncate the string to that index. 4. Return the resulting string. Time complexity is O(m) where m is the length of the username, as we scan the string multiple times. Space complexity is O(m) for the new string. This feeds into L0 by being called for each username in the list. Common mistakes include using the wrong order (e.g., handling plus before dots), not handling cases where plus is not present, or inefficient string concatenation. Real code pattern: def normalize(username): username = username.lower(); username = username.replace('.', ''); plus_index = username.find('+'); if plus_index != -1: username = username[:plus_index]; return username.

examples.trace: Step 1: lowercase: "a.b+c" -> "a.b+c". Step 2: remove dots: replace '.' with '' -> "ab+c". Step 3: find '+': index 2. Step 4: truncate at index 2: "ab". Output: "ab".
Step 1: lowercase: "A.B" -> "a.b". Step 2: remove dots: "ab". Step 3: find '+': not found (-1). Step 4: no truncation. Output: "ab".

ladders.title: Core Logic — Deduplication Using Hash Set
ladders.desc: Use a hash set to store unique normalized usernames and count them.
ladders.explanation: The core insight for solving the deduplication problem efficiently is to leverage a hash set data structure, which automatically handles uniqueness by its nature. The intuition is that instead of comparing each normalized username to all others (which would be O(n²)), we can insert each into a set that uses hashing to provide O(1) average-time insertions and lookups. As we process each username, we normalize it and add it to the set. If the normalized form is already in the set, the add operation has no effect; if it's new, it's added. After processing all usernames, the size of the set gives the count of unique normalized usernames. The algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty hash set. 2. For each username in the input list: a. Normalize the username (using the L1 subroutine). b. Add the normalized string to the set. 3. Return the size of the set. Time complexity is O(n * m) where n is the number of usernames and m is the average length, dominated by normalization. Space complexity is O(n * m) to store up to n normalized strings in the set. This connects to L0 as the main deduplication mechanism. Common mistakes include not normalizing before adding, using a list instead of a set, or misunderstanding set size. Real code pattern: set = set(); for username in usernames: normalized = normalize(username); set.add(normalized); return len(set).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize set: {}. Step 2: Process "a" -> normalize: "a" -> add to set: {"a"}. Step 3: Process "A" -> normalize: "a" -> add to set: {"a"} (already present, no change). Step 4: Process "a" -> normalize: "a" -> add to set: {"a"} (no change). Step 5: Return len(set) = 1.

Step 1: Initialize set: {}. Step 2: Process "alice" -> normalize: "alice" -> add to set: {"alice"}. Step 3: Process "bob" -> normalize: "bob" -> add to set: {"alice", "bob"}. Step 4: Return len(set) = 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — String Transformation Sequence
ladders.desc: Apply multiple string operations in a specific order to transform text.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves performing several string manipulations sequentially to achieve a desired transformation. The intuition is that string operations can be composed, but the order can affect the result, so we must carefully choose the sequence based on the problem requirements. For username normalization, we need to: 1. Convert to lowercase: This ensures case insensitivity by making all characters uniform. 2. Remove dots: This eliminates separator characters that don't affect identity. 3. Truncate at plus: This ignores optional suffixes. The order is crucial: we remove dots before truncating because dots after the plus should be ignored anyway, but dots before the plus need to be removed. If we truncated first, we might miss dots in the part before the plus. The algorithm: Start with an input string, apply lowercasing, then dot removal, then plus truncation. Time complexity is O(m) for scanning the string multiple times. Space complexity is O(m) for creating new strings. This feeds into L1 as the core of the normalization function. Common mistakes include incorrect order, not handling edge cases like empty strings or no plus sign, or using inefficient methods like repeated string concatenation in loops. Real code pattern: result = input_string.lower(); result = result.replace('.', ''); if '+' in result: result = result.split('+')[0]; return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: lowercase: "Test.Email+Extra" -> "test.email+extra". Step 2: remove dots: "testemail+extra". Step 3: find '+': index 8, truncate: "testemail". Output: "testemail".
Step 1: lowercase: "Simple" -> "simple". Step 2: remove dots: no dots, "simple". Step 3: find '+': not found, no truncation. Output: "simple".

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Processing in Strings
ladders.desc: Iterate through a string to perform operations like lowercasing or character removal.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves scanning each character in a string to apply transformations, such as converting to lowercase or filtering out specific characters. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we can process them character by character to build a new string with the desired properties. For example, to remove dots, we iterate through the original string, and for each character, if it is not a dot, we append it to a new string; if it is a dot, we skip it. Similarly, for lowercasing, we convert each character to its lowercase equivalent using a built-in function or ASCII manipulation. The algorithm for dot removal: Initialize an empty result string; loop through each character in the input string; if character != '.', append it to result; after loop, return result. Time complexity is O(m) where m is string length, as we examine each character once. Space complexity is O(m) for the new string. This feeds into L3 as a component of string transformation. Common mistakes include modifying strings in-place (strings are immutable in many languages), using inefficient concatenation in loops, or not handling special characters correctly. Real code pattern: result = ''; for char in input_string: if char != '.': result += char; return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize result = ''. Step 2: Iterate characters: 'a' -> not '.', append: result = 'a'. 'b' -> not '.', append: result = 'ab'. '.' -> is '.', skip. 'c' -> not '.', append: result = 'abc'. Step 3: Return "abc".
Step 1: Initialize result = ''. Step 2: Iterate: 't' -> append: 't'. 'e' -> append: 'te'. 's' -> append: 'tes'. 't' -> append: 'test'. Step 3: Return "test".

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Strings and Basic Operations
ladders.desc: Understand what strings are and how to perform simple operations like accessing characters or getting length.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: strings are sequences of characters, such as letters, digits, or symbols, used to represent text. In programming, strings are typically immutable, meaning once created, they cannot be changed; instead, operations create new strings. The intuition is that we can think of a string like an array of characters, where each character has a position (index) starting from 0. Basic operations include: getting the length of a string (number of characters), accessing a character at a specific index, and creating new strings through concatenation (joining) or slicing (extracting parts). For example, the string "hello" has length 5, character at index 0 is 'h', and slicing from index 1 to 3 gives "el". This foundation is essential for all higher-level string manipulations. The algorithm for basic access: Use string indexing (e.g., str[0]) to get characters, and len(str) to get length. Time complexity for these operations is O(1) typically. Space complexity depends on the string size. This feeds into L4 as the basis for character processing. Common mistakes include confusing string indices (starting at 0), trying to modify strings directly, or not understanding that strings are case-sensitive. Real code pattern: s = "example"; length = len(s); first_char = s[0]; substring = s[1:4].

examples.trace: Step 1: String is "cat". Step 2: Length: len("cat") = 3. Step 3: Characters: index 0: 'c', index 1: 'a', index 2: 't'.
Step 1: String is "" (empty). Step 2: Length: len("") = 0. Step 3: No characters to access.

approaches.name: Optimized Single Pass O(n * m)
approaches.summary: Process each username in a single pass with string building, applying normalization rules on-the-fly without creating intermediate strings for each step. Use a hash set for deduplication as in the previous approach, but with potential micro-optimizations in string handling.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Normalize & Deduplicate Usernames with Optimized Single Pass
ladders.desc: Complete solution implementing all normalization rules and deduplication in a single optimized pass.
ladders.explanation: This solution processes each username in a single pass while applying all normalization rules on-the-fly, avoiding intermediate string creations. The intuition is that we can build the normalized version character by character, applying rules as we go: convert to lowercase, skip dots, and ignore everything after a plus sign. By processing in a single pass, we minimize memory allocations and string operations. We maintain a hash set to store unique normalized usernames, giving us O(1) average lookup time. The algorithm iterates through each username, building the normalized version in a StringBuilder-like structure. For each character: if it's uppercase, convert to lowercase; if it's a dot, skip it; if it's a plus, stop processing the rest of the username; otherwise, add the character. After building the normalized string, add it to the hash set. Finally, return the size of the hash set. Time complexity is O(n * m) where n is number of usernames and m is average length. Space complexity is O(n * m) for storing unique normalized strings. Connection to L1: This uses the optimized normalization function from L1 as its core component. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like empty strings, multiple dots/pluses, or forgetting to reset state between usernames. Real code pattern: def normalize_usernames(usernames): unique = set(); for username in usernames: normalized = []; for ch in username: if ch == '+': break; if ch == '.': continue; normalized.append(ch.lower()); unique.add(''.join(normalized)); return len(unique).

examples.trace: Username 1: 'a.b+c' -> process 'a' (add 'a'), '.' (skip), 'b' (add 'b'), '+' (stop) -> normalized 'ab'. Username 2: 'ab' -> process 'a' (add 'a'), 'b' (add 'b') -> normalized 'ab' (already in set). Username 3: 'A.B' -> process 'A' (lowercase to 'a', add 'a'), '.' (skip), 'B' (lowercase to 'b', add 'b') -> normalized 'ab' (already in set). Unique set: {'ab'}. Count: 1.

Username 1: 'test.email+alex@leetcode.com' -> process 't','e','s','t' (add), '.' (skip), 'e','m','a','i','l' (add), '+' (stop) -> normalized 'testemail'. Username 2: 'test.e.mail+bob.cathy@leetcode.com' -> process 't','e','s','t' (add), '.' (skip), 'e' (add), '.' (skip), 'm','a','i','l' (add), '+' (stop) -> normalized 'testemail' (already in set). Username 3: 'testemail+david@lee.tcode.com' -> process 't','e','s','t','e','m','a','i','l' (add), '+' (stop) -> normalized 'testemail' (already in set). Unique set: {'testemail'}. Count: 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Optimized Single-Pass Normalization Function
ladders.desc: Function that normalizes a single username using on-the-fly character processing.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine takes a single username string and returns its normalized version by applying all rules in one pass through the characters. The intuition is to avoid creating multiple intermediate strings (like lowercasing the entire string, then removing dots, then truncating at '+'), which would require O(m) extra space and time for each operation. Instead, we process each character sequentially: if the character is '+', we immediately stop processing further characters (ignore everything after '+'). If it's '.', we skip it (do not add to output). If it's an uppercase letter, we convert it to lowercase before adding. Otherwise, we add the character as-is. We build the result using a list or StringBuilder to allow O(1) amortized appends. This approach ensures we only traverse the string once and build the result directly. Time complexity is O(m) where m is the length of the username. Space complexity is O(m) for the output string. Connection to L0: This function is called for each username in the main solution. Common mistakes include not breaking correctly on '+', incorrectly handling multiple special characters, or not resetting the output for each username. Real code pattern: def normalize_username(username): result = []; for ch in username: if ch == '+': break; if ch == '.': continue; result.append(ch.lower()); return ''.join(result).

examples.trace: Process 'a' -> add 'a'. Process '.' -> skip. Process 'b' -> add 'b'. Process '+' -> break. Result list: ['a','b'] -> string 'ab'.
Process 'A' -> lowercase to 'a', add 'a'. Process '.' -> skip. Process 'B' -> lowercase to 'b', add 'b'. Result list: ['a','b'] -> string 'ab'.

ladders.title: Core Logic — On-the-Fly String Processing with Early Termination
ladders.desc: Technique of processing string characters sequentially with conditional actions and early exit.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves traversing a string character by character, performing different actions based on each character, and potentially stopping early. The intuition is that by making decisions at each step, we can apply multiple transformations in a single pass without needing to revisit or create temporary strings. For this problem, the key actions are: 1) Stop processing when encountering a '+' (early termination), 2) Skip characters that are dots, 3) Convert uppercase to lowercase, and 4) Accumulate other characters. This is efficient because it minimizes operations: each character is examined once, and we only build the final string. The algorithm uses a loop over indices or characters, with if-else conditions for special cases. Time complexity is linear O(m). Space complexity is O(m) for the output. Connection to L1: This logic is the heart of the normalization function. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loops, not handling empty strings, or incorrect order of conditions (e.g., checking for '.' after '+'). Real code pattern: for ch in s: if ch == '+': break; elif ch == '.': continue; else: result.append(ch.lower()).

examples.input: "a.b+c" with rules: stop at '+', skip '.', lowercase
examples.trace: Index 0: 'a' -> not '+' or '.' -> lowercase 'a' -> add. Index 1: '.' -> skip. Index 2: 'b' -> not '+' or '.' -> lowercase 'b' -> add. Index 3: '+' -> break loop. Result: 'ab'.
Index 0: 'A' -> not '+' or '.' -> lowercase 'a' -> add. Index 1: '+' -> break loop. Result: 'a'. Dots and later characters ignored.

ladders.title: Building Block — Conditional Character Processing in Strings
ladders.desc: Iterating through a string and performing different actions based on character properties.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves looping through each character in a string and applying conditional logic, such as checking if a character matches certain criteria (e.g., being a specific symbol, uppercase, etc.). The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we can inspect each one to decide how to handle it. This is fundamental for text processing tasks. The algorithm uses a for-loop to access each character, often with an index or directly, and uses if-statements to test conditions. For example, we might check if ch == '.' or ch.isupper(). Based on the condition, we might skip, modify, or collect the character. Time complexity is O(m) for string length m. Space complexity depends on what we build; if collecting characters, O(m). Connection to L2: This is the general technique that L2 specializes with early termination and specific rules. Common mistakes include not considering all edge cases (like empty string), incorrect condition order, or modifying the string while iterating. Real code pattern: result = []; for ch in s: if condition1: action1; elif condition2: action2; else: default_action.

examples.input: String "a.B" with condition: if uppercase, convert to lowercase; if dot, skip; else keep
examples.trace: Character 'a': not uppercase, not dot -> keep 'a'. Character '.': dot -> skip. Character 'B': uppercase -> convert to 'b' -> keep 'b'. Result: 'ab'.

examples.input: String "123" with condition: if digit, keep; else skip
examples.trace: All characters are digits, so keep all. Result: '123'.

testCases.input: "a.B" with rules: lowercase uppercase, skip dots
"A+b" with rules: lowercase uppercase, stop at '+'
"A.A" with rule: lowercase uppercase, skip dots

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over String Characters
ladders.desc: Fundamental skill of accessing each character in a string sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves traversing a string from start to end, one character at a time. The intuition is that strings are indexed sequences, so we can use a loop to visit each position. This is essential for any string manipulation. The algorithm typically uses a for-loop: for ch in s: # process ch. Alternatively, use a while-loop with an index: i = 0; while i < len(s): ch = s[i]; i += 1. This allows us to examine or modify each character. Time complexity is O(n) for string length n. Space complexity is O(1) if not storing characters. Connection to L3: This iteration is the foundation for applying conditional processing. Common mistakes include going out of bounds (in while-loops), forgetting to increment index, or misunderstanding string immutability. Real code pattern: for character in my_string: print(character).

examples.trace: Loop iteration 1: ch = 'a'. Iteration 2: ch = 'b'. Iteration 3: ch = 'c'. End.
String is empty, loop does not execute.

testCases.expected: Characters: h e l l o
Characters: f o o   b a r

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Strings as Sequences of Characters
ladders.desc: Understanding that strings are ordered collections of characters that can be accessed individually.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers what strings are: sequences of characters like letters, digits, or symbols. The intuition is that a string is not just a single unit but made up of smaller parts (characters) arranged in a specific order. For example, the string "cat" consists of characters 'c', 'a', 't'. We can access individual characters using indices (positions starting from 0), so "cat"[0] is 'c'. Strings are typically immutable, meaning you cannot change a character directly, but you can create new strings. This concept is prerequisite for any string manipulation. Connection to L4: Knowing strings are sequences allows us to iterate over them. Common mistakes include confusing strings with lists, or trying to modify a string in-place. Real code pattern: s = "hello"; first_char = s[0] # 'h'.

examples.output: Length 3, characters: 'd' at index 0, 'o' at index 1, 'g' at index 2
examples.trace: String "dog" has 3 characters. Position 0: 'd'. Position 1: 'o'. Position 2: 'g'. We can access s[0] to get 'd'.
Empty string has no characters, so length is 0. Accessing any index would cause an error.

testCases.expected: Length 5, chars: a p p l e
Length 11, chars: h e l l o   w o r l d
